---
title: "Same report, every table framework"
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>",
                      message = FALSE, warning = FALSE, eval = FALSE)
```

```{r setup}
library(rtfreporter)
```

The R ecosystem for clinical tables is wonderfully diverse -- **rtables/tern**,
**tfrmt**, **gtsummary** (on its own or on a **cards/cardx** ARD), **Tplyr**,
**gt**, **flextable**, **huxtable** -- and teams pick whichever fits their style
and validation story. What they all have in common is the *last* step: a
**regulatory RTF** that opens cleanly in Word.

That last step is exactly what `rtfreporter` does, for **all of them**. This
article builds the **same demographics report from the same data** with several
frameworks and renders each to RTF through `rtfreporter`. The point is not that
one framework is best -- it is that **whatever you already use, rtfreporter is
the RTF back end**.

> The code below is shown for reference (`eval = FALSE`); each table's RTF is
> rendered offline and the Word output is shown as a screenshot. The full,
> runnable generator lives in
> [`data-raw/showcase_dm.R`](https://github.com/ichirio/rtfreporter/blob/main/data-raw/showcase_dm.R).

## The data

We use [`pharmaverseadam`](https://pharmaverse.github.io/pharmaverseadam/)'s
`adsl`, restricted to the three randomised arms, and summarise **age** (as `n`,
`Mean (SD)`, `Median`, `Min, Max`), an **age category**, **sex** and **race** by
treatment -- a production-style Table 14.1 demographics layout.

```{r data}
library(dplyr)
arm_levels <- c("Placebo", "Xanomeline Low Dose", "Xanomeline High Dose")
adsl <- pharmaverseadam::adsl |>
  filter(TRT01A %in% arm_levels,
         RACE %in% c("WHITE", "BLACK OR AFRICAN AMERICAN")) |>
  mutate(
    TRT01A = factor(TRT01A, levels = arm_levels),
    SEX    = factor(SEX, levels = c("M", "F"), labels = c("Male", "Female")),
    AGEGR  = cut(AGE, breaks = c(-Inf, 65, 81, Inf),
                 labels = c("<65", "65 - 80", ">80"), right = FALSE),
    RACE   = factor(RACE, levels = c("WHITE", "BLACK OR AFRICAN AMERICAN"),
                    labels = c("White", "Black or African American")))
arm_n <- table(adsl$TRT01A)
```

## The report furniture lives in rtfreporter

The running **header** (sponsor, protocol, page number and the table title) and
the **footer** (analysis note, program path and run time), the **Letter
landscape** page -- all of this is set **once, in rtfreporter**, and is identical
for every framework below. Only the *body* changes from framework to framework.

```{r furniture}
dm_header <- rtf_header(rows = list(
  c(l = "Acme Biopharma, Inc.", r = "Page {AUTO_PAGE} of {AUTO_TOTAL_PAGES}"),
  c(l = "Protocol ABC-2026-001", r = "Status: Draft"),
  c(c = "Table 14.1.1"),
  c(c = "Summary of Demographic and Baseline Characteristics"),
  c(c = "Safety Population"),
  c(c = "")))
dm_footer <- rtf_footer(rows = list(
  c(l = "Note: Percentages are based on the number of non-missing subjects in each treatment group."),
  c(l = "Program: /prod/abc/tfl/t_14_1_1.R", r = "Generated: 2026-06-17 09:14"),
  c(l = "Source: ADSL", r = "CONFIDENTIAL")))

# Every framework feeds its result to the same call.  `align_count_pct` lines
# up the "n (xx.x%)" cells; the generator also drops a blank row between groups
# and at the top / bottom (see data-raw/showcase_dm.R).
doc <- rtf_document(page = list(paper_size = "letter", orientation = "landscape")) |>
  rtf_section(page = 1, secinfo = list(header = dm_header, footer = dm_footer)) |>
  rtf_tables(as_rtftables(tbl, align_count_pct = TRUE))   # `tbl` = framework table
generate_rtfreport(doc, "dm.rtf", overwrite = TRUE)
```

# Demographics (DM)

## Build it once, let rtfreporter read it

These frameworks already carry the column labels, spanning headers, alignment
and cell formatting. `as_rtftables(read_meta = TRUE)` (the default) **reads that
metadata** straight off the object -- you do not re-specify the header.

### gtsummary

```{r gtsummary}
library(gtsummary)
tbl <- adsl |>
  select(TRT01A, AGE, AGEGR, SEX, RACE) |>
  tbl_summary(
    by = TRT01A,
    type = list(AGE ~ "continuous2"),                       # the 4-line age block
    label = list(AGE = "Age (years)", AGEGR = "Age category",
                 SEX = "Sex", RACE = "Race"),
    statistic = list(
      AGE ~ c("{N_nonmiss}", "{mean} ({sd})", "{median}", "{min}, {max}"),
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"),
    digits = list(AGE ~ list(N_nonmiss = 0, mean = 1, sd = 2,
                             median = 1, min = 0, max = 0))) |>
  modify_header(all_stat_cols() ~ "**{level}**  \nN = {n}")

as_rtftables(tbl, read_meta = TRUE)   # column labels + Ns read from gtsummary
```

```{r img-gtsummary, eval = TRUE, echo = FALSE, out.width = "100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/showcase/dm_gtsummary.png")
```

### gtsummary on a cards / cardx ARD

The modern, ARD-first workflow: compute an **Analysis Results Dataset** with
`cards`, summarise it with `gtsummary::tbl_ard_summary()`, then hand the table
to `rtfreporter` exactly as above.

```{r gtsummary-ard}
library(cards); library(gtsummary)
ard <- ard_stack(
  data = adsl, .by = TRT01A,
  ard_continuous(variables = AGE,
                 statistic = ~ list(N = length, mean = mean, sd = sd,
                                    median = median, min = min, max = max)),
  ard_categorical(variables = c(AGEGR, SEX, RACE)), .total_n = TRUE)

tbl <- tbl_ard_summary(
  ard, by = TRT01A,
  type = list(AGE = "continuous2"),
  label = list(AGE = "Age (years)", AGEGR = "Age category",
               SEX = "Sex", RACE = "Race"),
  statistic = list(AGE = c("{N}", "{mean} ({sd})", "{median}", "{min}, {max}"),
                   all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"))

as_rtftables(tbl, read_meta = TRUE)
```

```{r img-gtsummary-ard, eval = TRUE, echo = FALSE, out.width = "100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/showcase/dm_gtsummary_ard.png")
```

### rtables / tern

`tern::analyze_vars()` produces a `VTableTree`; `rtfreporter` reads its row
labels, column counts and spanning structure via `formatters::matrix_form()`.

```{r rtables}
library(rtables); library(tern)
lyt <- basic_table(show_colcounts = TRUE) |>
  split_cols_by("TRT01A") |>
  analyze_vars(
    vars = c("AGE", "AGEGR", "SEX", "RACE"),
    var_labels = c(AGE = "Age (years)", AGEGR = "Age category",
                   SEX = "Sex", RACE = "Race"),
    .stats = c("n", "mean_sd", "median", "range", "count_fraction"))
tbl <- build_table(lyt, adsl)

as_rtftables(tbl, read_meta = TRUE)
```

```{r img-rtables, eval = TRUE, echo = FALSE, out.width = "100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/showcase/dm_rtables.png")
```

### tfrmt

`tfrmt` is metadata-driven by design: a long summary plus a formatting spec,
rendered to `gt` with `print_to_gt()` -- which `rtfreporter` then reads.

```{r tfrmt}
library(tfrmt)
# `long` is a tidy summary: group / label / column / param / value
# (Age rows n / Mean / SD / Median / Min / Max, plus categorical n / pct).
spec <- tfrmt(
  group = group, label = label, column = column, param = param, value = value,
  body_plan = body_plan(
    frmt_structure(".default", ".default", frmt("xx.x")),
    frmt_structure("Age (years)", "n", frmt("xx")),
    frmt_structure(".default", "Mean (SD)",
                   frmt_combine("{Mean} ({SD})",
                                Mean = frmt("xx.x"), SD = frmt("xx.xx"))),
    frmt_structure(".default", "Min, Max",
                   frmt_combine("{Min}, {Max}", Min = frmt("xx"), Max = frmt("xx"))),
    frmt_structure(c("Age category", "Sex", "Race"), ".default",
                   frmt_combine("{n} ({pct}%)",
                                n = frmt("xx"), pct = frmt("xx.x")))))
g <- print_to_gt(spec, long)

as_rtftables(g, read_meta = TRUE)
```

```{r img-tfrmt, eval = TRUE, echo = FALSE, out.width = "100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/showcase/dm_tfrmt.png")
```

## Shape the body, set the look in rtfreporter

Some frameworks return just the **formatted body** -- the numbers, as strings --
and leave presentation to you. Here you do the opposite of the section above:
the framework transposes/summarises, and **`rtfreporter` supplies the column
header, alignment and grouping**.

### Tplyr

`Tplyr::build()` returns a data frame of formatted strings (plus ordering
helpers). We reshape it into a labelled body and pass the column header to
`rtftable()` ourselves.

```{r tplyr}
library(Tplyr)
built <- tplyr_table(adsl, TRT01A) |>
  add_layer(group_desc(AGE, by = "Age (years)") |>
              set_format_strings("n"         = f_str("xx", n),
                                 "Mean (SD)" = f_str("xx.x (xx.xx)", mean, sd),
                                 "Median"    = f_str("xx.x", median),
                                 "Min, Max"  = f_str("xx, xx", min, max))) |>
  add_layer(group_count(AGEGR, by = "Age category") |>
              set_format_strings(f_str("xx (xx.x%)", n, pct))) |>
  add_layer(group_count(SEX, by = "Sex") |>
              set_format_strings(f_str("xx (xx.x%)", n, pct))) |>
  add_layer(group_count(RACE, by = "Race") |>
              set_format_strings(f_str("xx (xx.x%)", n, pct))) |>
  build()

# ... reshape `built` into `disp`: a label column (group headers + indented
#     stats, a "Number of Subjects" row on top) and one column per arm ...

# Metadata lives HERE, in rtfreporter -- not in Tplyr:
col_header <- c("Characteristic", paste0(arm_levels, "\nN = ", arm_n))
rtftable(disp, col_header = col_header)
```

```{r img-tplyr, eval = TRUE, echo = FALSE, out.width = "100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/showcase/dm_tplyr.png")
```

## Two ways to divide the work

The two sections above are the key choice when wiring any framework to
`rtfreporter`:

| | **Build-and-read** | **Transpose-and-set** |
|---|---|---|
| Who owns the column header / spanning / formatting? | the table framework | `rtfreporter` |
| How rtfreporter gets it | `as_rtftables(read_meta = TRUE)` | you pass `col_header`, `col_spec`, ... |
| Best for | metadata-rich frameworks (rtables/tern, tfrmt, gtsummary) | body-only frameworks (Tplyr, tidytlg) |
| Trade-off | least code; depends on the framework's metadata | full control; you re-state the look once |

Both produce the **same RTF**. Pick whichever keeps the work where it already
lives: if your framework already knows the headers, let `rtfreporter` read them;
if it only knows the numbers, let `rtfreporter` dress them.

---

*Coming next: the same treatment for an **Adverse Events (AE)** summary (SOC / PT
with spanning headers), and additional frameworks (tidytlg, flextable,
huxtable).*
